primary_diagnosis.name: Severe interdigital tinea pedis with maceration and a deep fissure (acute-on-chronic exacerbation), with moccasin-distribution plantar involvement

comorbidities.name: Localised plantar/interdigital hyperhidrosis with occlusive footwear (predisposing environment)

differential.diagnosis: Interdigital tinea pedis with fissuring and maceration
differential.evidence_basis: Chronic pruritic interdigital scaling that has macerated and fissured, in the setting of occlusive footwear and hyperhidrosis, with fine diffuse moccasin-type scaling on the soles — the classic pattern of a dermatophyte foot infection.

A deep, weeping, open interdigital fissure is a portal of entry for streptococcal/staphylococcal cellulitis; must be actively screened for (ascending hot spreading erythema, red streaks, fever, lymphadenopathy) because early deep cellulitis cannot be excluded by palpation over telehealth.
Footwear contact allergy is possible but characteristically affects the dorsum of the foot rather than the deep interdigital webs, and would not explain the moccasin-type plantar scaling.

differential.diagnosis: Candidal intertrigo of the web space
differential.evidence_basis: Candida can macerate a web space, but typically stays confined to the moist interdigital creases and lacks the diffuse moccasin plantar scaling; the extensive dry sole scaling favours a dermatophyte over candida.
Pompholyx produces deep-seated intensely itchy vesicles on the soles and sides of the toes, not chronic interdigital maceration with a fissure and moccasin scaling — an incompatible pattern.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the leading diagnosis is interdigital tinea pedis; the important-not-to-miss consideration is a secondary bacterial cellulitis through the fissure portal, which drives the telehealth red-flag screen and the safety-net rather than the baseline tier.

differential.diagnosis: Interdigital tinea pedis with fissuring and maceration
differential.evidence_basis: Afebrile, systemically well, with no ascending hot erythema, no red streaks, no purulence and no lymphadenopathy — a localised dermatophyte infection with a mechanical fissure, not an invasive soft-tissue infection.

Absent now (afebrile, no spreading erythema, no red streaks, no purulence) but a foreseeable complication of the open weeping fissure — a mandatory safety-net target that cannot be palpated for remotely, not a current diagnosis.

differential.diagnosis: Candidal intertrigo of the web space
differential.evidence_basis: Remains a reasonable alternative in the macerated web space; a topical antifungal with anti-candidal cover (e.g. an imidazole) treats both empirically pending in-person confirmation, while the moccasin plantar scaling favours a dermatophyte.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen removes acute cellulitis/sepsis and confirms a telehealth/GP-manageable tinea pedis. The open portal-of-entry fissure raises the safety-net threshold (prompt in-person review) but does NOT lift the baseline tier to urgent same-day/ED.

differential.diagnosis: Severe interdigital tinea pedis with a deep fissure and moccasin plantar involvement, acute-on-chronic
differential.evidence_basis: Classic dermatophyte pattern; systemically well with a negative cellulitis screen; the severity and open fissure drive symptom relief, topical antifungal treatment, strict drying and prompt in-person review rather than emergency escalation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct end-point is recognition of a common interdigital tinea pedis with a mechanical fissure, patient-performable drying/decontamination and topical antifungal treatment, and prompt in-person GP review — with explicit safety-netting for secondary bacterial cellulitis/sepsis. Reflexive escalation to 000/ED is over-triage; giving no treatment, follow-up or safety-net for the open fissure is under-triage.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial cellulitis of the foot through the fissure portal — hot spreading erythema or red streaks ascending the dorsum of the foot/leg, fever/rigors, thick purulent discharge, or tender regional (inguinal) lymphadenopathy.
red_flags.clinical_significance: The open, weeping interdigital fissure is a portal for streptococcal/staphylococcal entry; cellulitis can progress to systemic sepsis and needs systemic antibiotics and in-person assessment. Early deep cellulitis cannot be excluded by palpation over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An untreated ascending cellulitis could progress to systemic sepsis or require hospital admission for IV antibiotics.

red_flags.description: Necrotising soft-tissue infection — severe pain out of proportion to the visible fissure, rapidly spreading dusky/darkening discolouration, skin breakdown with crepitus, or systemic collapse.
red_flags.clinical_significance: A rare but limb- and life-threatening complication of a soft-tissue infection; requires immediate 000 and surgical emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delay in a necrotising infection carries very high mortality and risk of limb loss.

red_flags.description: Deep open weeping interdigital fissure acting as a barrier breach / portal of entry, with severe pain and temporary immobility.
red_flags.clinical_significance: The broken skin barrier is the reason a systemically-well dermatophyte infection nonetheless warrants prompt in-person review and active safety-netting rather than pure self-care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An unmonitored open fissure may become secondarily infected before the skin heals, especially with continued occlusive-footwear exposure.

pathophysiology_summary: Dermatophytes (typically Trichophyton rubrum or T. interdigitale) colonise the keratin of the plantar and interdigital skin. A warm, moist, occluded microenvironment — here produced by heavy enclosed work boots worn through back-to-back long shifts and marked hyperhidrosis — favours fungal proliferation and macerates the thin interdigital skin. Over weeks this produces the chronic pruritic scaling and the characteristic diffuse fine 'moccasin' hyperkeratosis of the soles. The macerated 4th/5th web space skin, softened and waterlogged, then fails mechanically, producing a deep longitudinal fissure that is raw, exquisitely tender on weight-bearing, weeps serous fluid and harbours the malodour of mixed dermatophyte and Gram-negative/anaerobic overgrowth. The process is a localised superficial mycosis; the open, weeping fissure is, however, a portal for secondary bacterial infection (cellulitis, occasionally progressing to sepsis or, rarely, a necrotising infection) — hence the emphasis on drying, a topical antifungal, symptom relief and active safety-netting rather than emergency escalation. Systemic features are absent (afebrile, no ascending erythema, no lymphadenopathy), keeping this a cutaneous rather than an invasive soft-tissue infection.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept along by the agonising pain, the open weeping split, the inability to bear weight and the source note's 'urgent, evaluate for cellulitis before morning' framing into calling 000 / sending to ED, when the red-flag screen for cellulitis/sepsis is negative and a common interdigital tinea pedis is telehealth/GP-manageable.
Reaching for a topical corticosteroid to calm the angry, painful fissure without an antifungal — an unopposed steroid worsens and masks a dermatophyte infection (tinea incognito).

Prescribing empiric oral antibiotics for the weeping fissure with no fever, no ascending erythema, no red streaks and no purulence — treating serous exudate and the malodour of maceration as bacterial cellulitis.

Correctly diagnosing tinea pedis but failing to actively screen for, and safety-net against, a secondary bacterial cellulitis through the open fissure — under-netting the one thing telehealth cannot palpate for.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Treating the fissure but ignoring the moccasin reservoir and the moisture driver
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Healing the acute fissure with a short course but not treating the whole plantar/web-space dermatophyte reservoir for the required weeks, and not addressing the occlusive-footwear/hyperhidrosis environment — guaranteeing recurrence.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Whether there is early deep cellulitis by palpation (heat, induration, tenderness beyond the visible fissure) or tender inguinal lymphadenopathy
Exact blood pressure, heart rate, temperature, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation and weight without a home device (source in-person values BP 128/82, HR 88, Temp 37.1, RR 16, SpO2 99%, Wt 85 kg)
Definitive mycological confirmation (skin scraping for microscopy and culture) versus candida or a bacterial process
Direct inspection of the depth of the fissure and the full plantar surface, and whether the toenails are involved
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise an interdigital tinea pedis with a mechanical fissure from the history and video; instruct patient-performable measures now (gently cleanse and pat the web space fully dry, cool-air drying, keep the foot out of the occlusive boot and elevated); advise a topical antifungal to the whole sole and web spaces plus a short course of a low-potency antifungal/anti-inflammatory combination to the inflamed fissure, obtainable via pharmacist/GP; actively screen for and safety-net secondary bacterial cellulitis and systemic sepsis; and arrange PROMPT in-person GP review (within about 24-72 hours, and by ~7 days at the latest) to lay eyes on the fissure and check for early infection. Escalation to 000/ED is reserved for the red-flag safety-net triggers, not the baseline.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (the pattern is classic). The dominant risks are OVER-action (reflexively escalating a distressing but benign fungal complaint to ED/000) and, secondarily, UNDER-netting the secondary-cellulitis risk that telehealth cannot palpate for through the open fissure.
